speaker: interviewer
text: --on December 17, 1996 in Des Plaines, Illinois, interviewed by Leah Shifrin Averick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I, I'd like to go back to something that happened during when I was fairly young-- I mean, maybe seven, eight years. And it must have happened, like, around 1936, '37. My father owned a reconvalescent home-- sanatorium, in, in the mountain resort that Hitler also later had his-- had his retreat house. And it was called Obersalzberg. That's O-B-E-R-S-A-L-Z-B-E-R-G, which means mountain, 'salt mountain berg'-- salt mountain. And that's near Berchtesgaden, which is a very lovely, scenic resort in the Bavarian Alps. He had this-- this was a perfect match for my father because he had the clinic in Berlin. And after people were recuperating, he would send them, if they could afford it, [LAUGHS] to the sanatorium in the Bavarian Alps. So that made perfectly good sense to have this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used that-- we used to go there for vacation. The couple that-- the husband and wife couple that ran this were very loyal people that thought very highly and respected my dad. So I remember being there in the summer of-- it had to be, like, '35, 1935. I guess I was just eight years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when Hitler had already moved to his house, which was above ours on the mountain--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same area?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same area, same, same area, the same mountain. It was just a half an hour walk. So-- and word spread that he was, was there in the summertime. And he would greet tourists and shake hands and talk to people and try to be popular.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the governess that we had decided to-- my mother didn't go. But my-- the governess decided-- and that he is very fond of children. So he-- we went up there-- That's what they-- that Hitler--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the-- that was the word that, that was, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Out.

speaker: interviewee
text: --spread, that, that Hitler was very fond of children, liked children. And he would always talk to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did go up there. And it was strange. We had no idea what was going to happen. I mean, eight years old, we don't know anything about persecution or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here is this man. And he was not-- as far as I remember, he was not in uniform. He was in civilian clothes. And he was talking very friendly. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside his house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside, yeah, he was standing outside and talking to people. Now, I-- people didn't-- people didn't go inside the house. But he was standing out and receiving people, long lines of visitors, and so forth--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shaking--

speaker: interviewee
text: --shaking hands, talking to-- particularly talking, you know, very, very fondly to children. So that's my-- [LAUGHS] was our first-- not knowing what this-- what this man would-- the, the destruction and, and evil that-- and bad situations that this man would later cause--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --cause us.

speaker: interviewer
text: --'38, after your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, something like this, yeah. And then-- yeah, that was then. And I'm trying to think what the, the-- oh, my next experience seeing Hitler in person was in-- I believe it was in June of 1941, during the war. Germany had already overrun the Netherlands, Denmark, Belgium, Norway. And now they had total victory in France. And they had, had captured Paris. And so the, the divisions that had to-- the German Army divisions that had captured Paris were coming to Berlin for a victory parade down the main boulevard. And all the schools-- grade schools, high schools-- everybody was required, class by class-- the entire school went to line the parade road. So we, [LAUGHS] we wound up standing almost immediately directly across from the reviewing stand where Hitler and his high officers and, and, and rest of his government were standing and reviewing the parade. And the thing that struck me-- you know, we were supposed to cheer, too. Every time the flags would come by, you know, we were-- everybody cheered. But the thing that amazed me was that Hitler was constantly standing and holding up his arm like this and never-- that was like a four-, five-hour parade. I mean, we were all getting pretty darn tired.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, this man was standing there. I, I can't remember if he ever left his arm down, which we were starting-- we were starting to make jokes about it. I mean, is he a Superman? How can anybody be like this? Maybe he's a robot, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody suggested he might have a spring installed under his-- [LAUGHS] in his armpit that he would be able. But it certainly was very impressive. It was certainly impressive to the German people and the-- walking by of all those German Army divisions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about you as a young boy who'd been, you know, who'd been called a Jew, or whatever you were called at school, how, how were you feeling towards this man was implementing-- who had implemented all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very-- not very friendly. I mean, I was-- you know, but I have no choice. I had to be there. If I wasn't going to be there, you couldn't, you know, just duck out and-- unless you played sick or something. And that, you know-- and people would not believe it. But I remember that it was difficult. I-- a very ironic thing was that I became friendly-- because we lived in the same neighborhood, and we had to walk back-- back and forth-- back and to school every day together. That was in-- you know, this is not like today, when children are constantly driven to school. You walk and you walk miles and this Oberschule was quite a few miles. And it was tough in the wintertime. But I got friendly with a, a boy. His name-- last name was Lorenz, L-O-R-E-N-Z. I think his first name was Erich. And he was two years older, actually. I don't know why he was still in my class. I don't know if he had been left-- you know, through-- what do you say, over, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Left behind, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Left behind, yeah, for a year or so. Anyway, his father was a top SS Gropenfuhrer, or-- almost like a Gauleiter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's a Gropenfuhrer?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Gropenfuhrer is like a, a command-- is like a-- is almost like a Lieutenant General, something like this. They had-- they had different designations for officers ranked in the SS, Waffen SS, which is the military branch of the SS which is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: You call that Waffen SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Waffen means actually 'arms,' you know, translated. It's W-A-F-F-E-N, Waffen SS. That's the-- these were the-- they were later then also used in the extermination of Jews in all the occupied countries. But he was a-- his father was, was a top-- I think he was in charge of all of occupied Poland. And so the kid was extremely rich. He lived in an apartment. And-- but we would-- I would go over there and play with him. And he knew who I was. And he did not-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you know, he, he would not talk disparagingly to me or would insult me. He just took me as a-- as a-- as a person, as a playmate.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had become really, you know, fairly friendly because walking back and forth every day to school. And I was-- and one day, we were coming back from school. And he said to me, do you know what I would really like to do at this very moment? And I said, no. And he said to me, you know, I would like to have an automatic weapon and shoot everybody in sight on that street here. I said-- I looked at him in awe. I said, how the heck can you say that? These are your own fellow Germans. I mean, you're not in Poland, where, where your father is. And he might be doing that to the people in Poland. He said no, I just feel the-- I just feel the urge to do this. That would satisfy him. I said to him, what would you do with me? And he said, no, I wouldn't shoot you because you're my friend. And needless to say that this kid, you know, he had everything. He had leather topcoats. And his father probably had things that they had from Jew-- that they had, you know, taken from, from the concentration camps when-- where people were-- you know, where Jews were exterminated. I don't think any-- I don't think he had-- I don't know if he had jewelery. But they were extremely rich and well-off, you know? How did you see that beside the leather coats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- the-- how, how did I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that they were rich? What, what did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh they lived in a very exquisite, exclusive apartment. And they had everything. And nobody had anything-- nobody had enough food. Nobody had enough chocolate and candy, and, and any of these luxury food items. They were unknown. Everything was so strictly rationed, you know. They had everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, they were a, a special, privileged class.

speaker: interviewer
text: The SS--

speaker: interviewee
text: All the-- yeah. And their--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or him?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and their families, their members, sure, their family members. So I don't know why I became friendly. It just was a coincidence, you know. We-- he didn't-- he, he liked me. And we just were walking back and forth. But I was very curious to find out-- after I didn't see him anymore after I left school. After the war, I found out what had happened to him. He had enlisted into the SS young officers school. And he was sent to the-- and because he was older, so he was already sent to the war. I think he was sent to-- in 1944, '45. And he was captured by the Russians. That's the last I heard. And the Russians never really took SS officers, you know. They usually shot them, you know. So I guess that's where he wound up, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, I mean, his state of mind-- is he crazy? I mean, is he insane? What, what, what kind of a person is he that likes to, you know, go around and shoot people, innocent people, his own people? I mean, I-- I mean, in-- the Nazi [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] idea of thinking was you can kill all the other people, but don't call your own-- don't kill your own people. But that's what he did, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and so this is-- went on for a long time. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean your friendship with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that went on. But then another thing was-- and it did-- it did cause me all kinds of problems. I mean, because all my-- like I said, all the-- my other classmates knew I was Jewish. They would not have interfered with my-- given my homework and when I-- when I-- to recite, they would shout me down. And just--

speaker: interviewer
text: In front of the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: In front of the teachers--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the teachers didn't stop--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the teacher was sitting there, you know, and without doing any intervening. I mean, it was no. He's not going to do anything. Because the teacher had a party button on his-- on his lapel. So he's not going to-- even if he was no ardent Nazi, that-- he wasn't going to, you know, go to bat for me. So, so, he was just-- it was probably the best that I did not continue going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother? Did you hear-- exchange stories with your brother? What was going on with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to each other? Did it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we talked, but he didn't-- he was two years younger. So he was not in the same-- in the same environment that I was in. But, you know, I couldn't even-- I couldn't even have a nice playtime, leisure time. I was-- when I-- after school, when I wanted to go out and, you know, ride on my bike-- we lived in a-- we lived then-- by that time, we had recovered from the clinic, you know. Had-- our, our financial, economic situation had stabilized. So we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: In another apartment--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were in a-- we were in another nice-- yeah, we were in a nice apartment at that time when I went to school. Because you, of course, go to school wherever your-- in what district you live in. So we were-- no, we were well-- we were well-situated then. But as a-- it still-- I, I, you know, I could not-- it was difficult. The, the issue of being half-Jewish would come up anytime. Because now, at an age when you're 14, 15, 16 years old, people think. I mean, you're more mature. And then, then you think about the differences in-- between races and between-- and between different people, you know, the-- what makes people different. So my classmates would always taunt me. And, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Taunt you how?

speaker: interviewee
text: Make-- you know, make, make insulting remarks to me. And I remember one day when I had just gotten a new bicycle-- which was quite an accomplishment because you couldn't get any more bicycles during the war. They were used [LAUGHS] for the military, too, for some reason. So that was, like, '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow my mother had gotten a very nice new bike for me. And I was riding around, trying to, to show it off to my classmates. And they were all insulting me and called, called it-- that this is-- what are you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're riding Abraham's sled, you know, in, in, [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, in, in German. So I-- that was not very complimentary and very nice of them. But I had to live with these kind of things. So I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Continually you had these taunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, these things would never stop. So it probably was for the best. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: For the best that you what?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I left. It was for the best that I probably left school because the humiliation and embarrassment would have only grown more stronger, you know, after that. Because I remember the, the Hitler Youth movement and the victories that the German Army had during the early years of the war really contributed to this atmosphere of self-assurance and of overwhelming victorious feelings, you know. I remember when I was in high school-- until I got kicked out-- that the-- you know, they-- even over the intercom system on the-- in schools, they would put on the special announcement that there was another big victory in the North Atlantic that had sank so many more American or British fighters, you know. And so then there-- it was almost like a short celebration every time. And people would take a break from your program. And they would all go-- and I remember in the class where I was in, they would all run to the-- to the map-- where the big map was and pinpointing the situation where the, the, the attack was by the German submarines. And then they would all say, oh, well it's only a matter of time when Germany wins the war. I-- a very interesting memory, there was one young blonde kid. He looked very German, was young. His name was-- his last name was Krafft. And he had been born in Cleveland. And it strikes me so funny because I know that the Krafts-- that's big-- of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cheese.

speaker: interviewee
text: --it's just a coincidence that Kraft Food manufacturer. But he was American-born. But his father was-- both parents were German. And they came-- returned to Germany just as the war-- before-- well, just before the US ended the war. And this kid was more, more fanatic a Nazi than anyone in that class. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he show it? How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: He showed it by constantly saying that-- how, how, how we-- how he hopes that Germany defends the-- defeats-- wins the war and defeats the United States and occupies the United States, and that he would have a big part in it because he speaks fluent English. So he was-- he was worse than any one of the others. I could not believe that, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was-- then after I left, left school, I, I I had-- what was I going to do with my life now? I had no education. My mother tried to enroll us into a, a Jesuit school, a private school. They-- she figured-- she thought they might-- she had heard that they might take somebody like us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They actually accepted us. But then they changed their mind because they had gotten some calls from the Gestapo. And they said-- and, and asked them, and, and asked them to supply their student lists. So they got cold feet, couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every student list listed your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. What they have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your race?

speaker: interviewee
text: --every student list, whatever your race on there. So that's how they would find out. So they were-- they were going to do it. But they, they changed their mind because they were afraid that the Nazis would, you know, then close them down and probably send them to a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't know what-- we didn't know what to do. I mean, wasting time, you know. So she finally found-- I couldn't go to any. I tried other private schools. Nobody would take us. I mean-- Because you were a Mischling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Half-Jewish, a Mischling, yeah. So finally, my mother found somebody, a private teacher, who was teaching shorthand and typewriting. And she-- and he actually risked his, his safety with that. He took a-- he took a big risk by accepting me. And he taught me in his apartment. And I-- so it's so ironic because I didn't-- I don't really ever master the shorthand. But I did learn how to typewrite by the blind, regular system. And I had so little of an idea that this, later on in my life, would be the, the one function that would become my-- the major portion-- major element of my-- of my life, making my, my profession-- my professional work as a writer, as a-- as an author, as a public-relations person. I mean, I had no idea. But this-- I did learn this perfectly, you know. And it was just great. But this man risked, risked his-- took a very strong risk. And I, I don't-- I don't remember his name. But I will always be grateful to him that he was-- that he was gracious enough to accept me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you sneak into his apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, no--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the morning, at night--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think it was late in the afternoon. It was in the wintertime. And I think it was dark already. He was-- it was not-- no, I didn't really think that somebody would-- I didn't think somebody would watch him or watch me. I mean, that I-- we didn't assume. But then the next step after Herta's, I had to do something else. So my uncle, Arno Sommerfeld, knew some-- somebody out in the-- in one of the other suburbs of Berlin who ran an insurance business and who was a member of the Nazi party. I don't know. I don't what recall all the details-- and who could use, use somebody as an office clerk. And, you know, everybody had to work, you had to register with the-- with the employment department. Nobody, nobody, from a certain age-- I was then, I'm trying to think, 1943, 16 years old, you know-- then I-- you had to work. So he took me in as an officer. It was just a-- just as a-- it was a farce. I mean, it was just a, a pretend job because I was really not at work. It turned out-- I, I think that the man knew-- he knew, of course, who I was. He wanted to already buy some insurance for the-- for the-- expecting the time when Germany lost the war. And so he can say, well, I helped somebody. So that makes me a good guy, you know. No, I mean, really, seriously. A lot of people tried to build this-- build this kind of insurance for themselves that were Nazi party and had the foresight to see that Germany is going to lose the war, and that they're all going to be held accountable for what has--

speaker: interviewer
text: And by then--

speaker: interviewee
text: --what has gone on.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that year-- by that year? Excuse me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --was just-- no, that was just before that. There's still--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was '43. Yeah, but actually, I have to go back for a  moment. My father really died in-- a normal death because of his asthma. And he had-- and he suffered a sepsis from an operation in April of 1937, which was a-- probably a fortunate, in a sense, that he was spared the persecution, especially in the terrible disappointment that-- because he had thought that this persecution would never happen, would never, never take place. And then to realize, so-- 1938, a year later when this all happened. So he, he died in the-- in the-- not knowing what, what terrible things would happen after had he lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And did he-- did you witness Kristallnacht? Where were you when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kristallnacht, I was in Berlin. But I-- [DRINKS] I was there. But I, I didn't see-- I wasn't, you know-- that's happened during the night. And I, I didn't see that. I was not-- I was too young to be allowed-- to go outside-- to go out at night. But we saw the damage the next day and, of course, heard it on the radio--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I just saw some broken windows. I didn't see anybody being, you know, mistreated or, you know, or beaten, I didn't see that. I mean-- and they don't really-- played that down. I mean, they didn't-- they didn't-- the Nazi propaganda machine wouldn't say, we beat them and tortured or we-- or we-- and even killed somebody. They just said, these people are responsible for all the bad things that have happened in Germany. We're just paying them back. So we're destroying their stores and synagogues, and so forth. They didn't really talk about that they killed anybody. See, they never-- that's why-- talk about the concentration camps, was-- you know about what was going on in concentration camps, really, they played that down. That, that was one of the things that you couldn't-- that nobody wanted to-- was, was allowed to talk about, and nobody wanted to talk about, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And even with, with your neighbors moving away, Jewish neighbors moving away-- or you weren't aware of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just kept it quiet. Nobody, nobody dared to talk about it. I mean, only if you-- if you knew somebody who, who, who was not going to report you and who was-- or maybe thought-- felt the same-- was thinking the same way, felt the same way as you are that you could trust. Otherwise you never talked to people like this. And so then the-- the thing-- you know, so I went to this job in the-- in this-- out in that suburb. And then the air raids started to become more and more severe every, every night. These were mostly done by the RAF-- night bombing. And they tried-- the idea-- the goal was saturation bombing and trying to, to destroy the morale of the German people, you know, and so trying to get from the inside to try and to undermine Hitler's iron grip on the country. And, of course, that was having certainly a strong impact. I mean, those air raids-- first, the clinic that we had was bombed out. And this woman that had been governess and later became the administrator--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your governess?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our governess and who had been our-- and then later was our-- and later, my, my mother, because she was a reliable and loyal person, pulled her into the clinic to help her with these-- with the chief nurse to put up a front against that Nazi doctor. She died in that. That was a terrible-- I mean, I still remember that that was an awful air raid. I mean, it was one of the worst. We were sitting in-- every house had, you know, shelters, air-raid shelters. And we were sitting there. And it was just-- everything was-- bombs were just raining around us. And practically a whole quarter of the city was on fire with these incendiary bombs. They just-- there's no way to stop them, you know. Water doesn't put, put them out. You just let it burn. And so we have-- get back to the-- to the apartment. Our house-- our building wasn't-- apartment building wasn't, wasn't hit, but pretty much around us. We got a phone call. And it said, come immediately. He said, the clinic is-- was total direct hit, every-- a lot of casualties. No, no, no public transportation-- we were running, walking five miles and-- to the clinic. And what a picture. I mean, the whole place is torn down. The emergency crews with, with, you know, big lights-- emergency lights on, digging out the people. And this governess, this-- who, who was among the people that were killed. She was in the large elevator where they have gurneys trying to pick people-- putting people down from the upper floors when the bomb hit. And that called-- it was a five-story building. The whole shaft fell on top of the large elevator. Everybody who was in there was, you know, killed. And many other people in the hospital were killed. That was a night. So I came back. And I put up-- and, and we, we were trying to protect, standing there, because there were people who started stealing immediately, too, you know, when you have a situation like this. So-- and they had-- they had the air-raid wardens that they were-- so I don't know how I got a helmet, a regular steel helmet, from one of those air-raid wardens. And I put them-- and I put them up. And my mother said--

speaker: interviewer
text: You put them on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why don't you stand at the end? They have, yeah, they have-- no, not the-- it had no swastika. It was just an-- it was just a regular, plain helmet. But the air-raid wardens used them. So I don't know how I got it. And I, I stayed the-- and I tried to stand guard at the entrance-- at the drive-- at the driveway for the-- to the hospital. And my mother came back and says, I think you better take that helmet off. You look like you serve in the Jewish brigade. You know, that-- they had a Jewish brigade in, in, in Palestine then, you know, that was fighting with the British. So I said-- I said that that might not be a good idea to, to, to do this, you know. What really had happened-- the reason they did not purposely hit the clinic. There was a-- there was a, a miss-- a big miss bombing raid. They-- the large--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was an American bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were British. The large-- the, the telephone center for all of Berlin was down the street. It was a large building that had all the-- all the switchboards, you know, all the connections for every-- for every telephone connection in Berlin. That's what they had targeted. And they missed. I mean, it's not like the smart bombs that they have today. So they hit everything around it, including the clinic. They did not hit purposely. So-- and then six months later we lost our apartment. These air raids-- these devastating air raids continued. And that's when we left. We left-- we left-- after we lost our, our home, we went to Silesia and tried to-- and because we had friends there. And we tried to find a new-- and moved there. Because there was nothing. We couldn't get any apartment. Everything was-- so many were bombed out. So we went there. And we had friends there. And they put us in, in temporary housing. And that's when I had to, of course, to, to report immediately. Because now it was-- that was the end of 1943-- report to the police department again, fill out those forms again, identify myself as what I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a Mischling, you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right. And then I had to-- and also that I'm-- that I'm also available for any work because that's-- that, that goes without saying that you had to-- that was the, the mandatory. So I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, too, had to register?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, too, had to and my brother, too. But I was the main object. So-- and, that, that was an-- that's another chapter which I will I, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --can later more discuss in detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they, they all registered you-- all registered--

speaker: interviewee
text: All registered--

speaker: interviewer
text: --at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --police department--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And then they-- but then they would find me work. And I would get a notification that I have to, you know, report for work for--

speaker: interviewer

speaker: interviewer
